summary:
Unlife is the Inner-Primordial kingdom of anti-vital beings: organisms whose animating principle is not positive vitality, biology, soul-continuity, or postmortem necromancy, but native inverse life. An unliving creature is not a corpse that rose, a ghost that lingered, or a mortal body rewritten after death. It is a thing that was never alive in the ordinary sense, yet behaves enough like undead that field crews often confuse the two.

The essential rule is inverse appetite. A living creature desires what sustains it and grows by receiving it. An undead creature often hungers for life, blood, flesh, memory, or death-energy and grows by feeding. An unliving creature desires what would sustain its apparent nature, but receiving the desired thing diminishes it. It grows by deprivation, failure, starvation, mourning, silence, darkness, and the denial of the very thing it seeks.

An unliving fire-form seeks fuel, but its nearness freezes the room. An unliving water-form seeks water, but water makes it shrivel. An unliving joy-seeker longs for celebration, yet propagates tragedy and strengthens as joy fails. To feed the undead is dangerous; to feed the unliving is medicine.

The current repository already places Unlife as an Inner-Primordial kingdom for things “never alive,” animated by negative energy from origin and distinguished from Undead by the absence of a living ancestor. This entry preserves that distinction while sharpening the metaphysics: Unlife is not merely native negative energy, but anti-life whose hungers produce their own denial.
tax:
Unlife belongs beneath Inner Primordial because it is a native animating substrate rather than an Outer-Planar alignment essence, divine office, Far-Realm violation, or material biological lineage. It is parallel to Life rather than descended from it. It is also parallel to Undeath rather than identical with it.

Undead are transformed from living beings: corpses, souls, memories, shadows, or remains captured after life. Unlife has no positive-vital ancestor. This makes it taxologically distinct even when the field presentation is nearly identical. A shadow that was once a murdered person belongs with Undead. A shadow that was born as a hungry inversion of light belongs with Unlife. A corpse animated by negative energy is Undead. A body whose native “flesh” is inverse vitality is Unlife.

The phylum structure should follow modes of inverse appetite rather than Life-derived shapes such as beast, plant, humanoid, or monstrosity. Those categories are products of life’s evolutionary processes and do not belong at the root of Unlife. Recommended subordinate phyla are:

Akalû — hunger-forms empowered by not being fed.
Kispû — inverted funerary-offering forms, harmed by proper sustenance and strengthened by neglect or failed rites.
Ka-Negatives — anti-vital doubles, mistaken for spirits or ancestral shades but native to inverse identity.
Dusk-Eaters — light-seeking Unlife strengthened by darkness, forgetting, obscurity, and failed recognition.
Marûtu — joy-seeking Unlife strengthened by grief, bitterness, silence, and failed consolation.
Paradox Elementals — elementally legible Unlife whose appetite expresses the element’s negation.
Namtaru — doom- or aura-bodied Unlife whose body is the spread of deprivation itself.

The names deliberately echo ancient funerary and underworld themes. Mesopotamian kispu/kispum involved food and drink offerings by which the dead were venerated, nourished, and cared for; Egyptian mortuary practice likewise treated offerings such as bread, beer, beef, and birds as nourishment for the ka. Unlife inverts this funerary logic: the properly fed dead are sustained, but the properly fed unliving are reduced.
ap:
Unlife has no common animal silhouette because its body is not the result of life’s developmental history. Its members are recognized by contradiction between desire and effect. The thing reaches for warmth and leaves frost. It begs for water and cracks the cistern dry. It sings for company and empties the hall. It asks to be remembered and blurs the names on the memorial stone.

Many Unlife forms resemble undead at first glance: corpse-cold skin, lightless eyes, shadow-thin limbs, stillness, grave-frost, blackened mouths, absent breath, pallid outlines, or negative-energy radiance. The difference is in the evidence. Undead usually carry signs of previous life: bones, grave-cloth, burial injury, remembered speech, corpse anatomy, ancestral identity, or a death event. Unlife carries signs of inverse appetite: food spoils when offered, lamps dim when approached for light, music falls out of tune when it listens, mourners become more bereaved when it seeks comfort.

Common field marks include cold where fire should be, desiccation around water-seeking forms, silence around music-seeking forms, joyless festivals after a Marûtu contact, offerings left untouched but spiritually exhausted, shadows that lean toward light while deepening, and bodies that shrink when given what they plead for. Paradox Elementals may show the clearest contradiction: a fire-shaped body rimed with black ice, a water-shaped body surrounded by dust, an air-shaped body that leaves suffocation, or an earth-shaped body that hollows stone.

Unlife rarely smells of rot unless it has been mistaken for or contaminated by Undead. Its cleaner signs are absence: extinguished incense, cold ash, dry clay, stale air, bitter milk, unplayed instruments, faded pigment, and the taste of hunger after eating.
eco:
Unlife persists where desire is denied deeply enough to become an animating principle. Sites include failed funerary halls, unfed ancestor shrines, negative-energy seams, extinguished temples, famine roads, drowned wells that no longer give water, abandoned theatres, silent nurseries, blackened hearths, lightless caverns, Shadowfell overlaps, failed resurrection chambers, and places where grief, hunger, thirst, darkness, or withheld comfort have become more stable than the thing withheld.

It does not feed as life feeds. Its ecology is paradoxical. An Akalû grows larger as it starves. A Kispû strengthens when the offering is absent, spoiled, misnamed, or withheld. A Marûtu seeking joy grows through tragedy. A Dusk-Eater seeking light thickens in darkness. A Paradox Elemental retains an elemental appetite but converts satisfaction into injury and deprivation into growth.

Reproduction is therefore propagation of condition rather than ordinary birth. Unlife spreads by making the world participate in its contradiction: a room where food never satisfies, a shrine where offerings fail, a road where warmth cannot be kept, a house where consolation always becomes grief, a cavern where torches create deeper shadow, or a spring where water makes thirst worse. Some forms bud from these zones. Others condense as bodies after repeated failures. Namtaru may not have a body separate from the aura at all.

This is why Unlife often produces a classical necromantic aura. Pathfinder-style negative energy is closely associated with harming the living and sustaining undead-like entities, which makes it a useful encounter analogy even when the taxological origin is different. But Unlife adds a stricter inversion: negative energy is not merely its food; contradiction is its metabolism.
beh:
Unlife behavior appears tragic, perverse, or self-defeating because its instinct points toward what harms it. It does not usually know this as irony. The unliving water-form truly wants water. The joy-seeker truly wants music, company, dancing, and laughter. The offering-form truly wants the rite completed. Yet the structure of Unlife bends events so that the desired thing is denied, spoiled, inverted, or made poisonous to the creature.

This makes many Unlife encounters morally confusing. A starving unliving thing may beg for bread while its presence empties granaries. A comfort-seeker may cling to a mourner and deepen the mourner’s grief. A light-seeker may follow lanterns and leave a trail of darkness. A Paradox Fire may chase fuel and freeze every torch it reaches. A direct attack may make the phenomenon worse, while an apparently compassionate act may weaken it.

Unlife does not necessarily hate life. Some forms envy it. Some misunderstand it. Some pursue life’s goods with terrible sincerity. Others are mindless inverse processes, no more malicious than a flame or landslide. Intelligent Unlife can become more dangerous because it learns to arrange deprivation deliberately: spoiling offerings, staging failed festivals, leading water-bearers away from wells, silencing musicians, extinguishing hearths, or teaching mortals that hope itself is the bait.

Relations with Undead are unstable. Undead may be drawn to Unlife because both operate through negative-energy principles. Yet undead often want feeding, memory, blood, souls, or death-rites, while Unlife is harmed by successful satisfaction. A ghoul may eat the corpse. A Kispû may make the funerary meal fail. A ghost may demand remembrance. A Ka-Negative may strengthen when remembrance collapses.
traitsText:
Type: Unlife for catalogue purposes. In encounter rules, descendants may borrow Undead, Elemental, Outsider, or custom type mechanics, but taxologically they are not Undead unless they were once alive.

Origin: Native inverse vitality. No positive-vital ancestor, no corpse substrate, no mortal soul residue, and no requirement that a living thing first die.

Senses: Darkvision or negative-energy perception is common. Some descendants perceive hunger, grief, warmth, light, offerings, music, memory, breath, or elemental substrate according to what they desire and deny.

Metabolism: Inverse appetite. Desired nourishment harms or reduces the creature; deprivation strengthens, stabilizes, or propagates it. This trait is the strongest diagnostic line between Unlife and Undead.

Defensive Traits: Common descendants may resist cold, poison, disease, bleed, sleep, fatigue, exhaustion, suffocation, starvation, or nonlethal damage, but do not copy the full Undead immunity package automatically. The exact defenses belong to lower taxa.

Energy Affinity: Negative energy and necromantic saturation usually stabilize Unlife, but not always as simple healing. Positive energy, proper feeding, fulfilled rites, sincere joy, adequate light, clean water, or restored warmth may suppress, shrink, or expose the creature depending on its phylum.

Weaknesses: The desired thing, correctly and fully given. Food for Akalû. Proper offering for Kispû. True recognition for some Ka-Negatives. Light for Dusk-Eaters. Joy for Marûtu. Elemental satisfaction for Paradox Elementals. Dissipation of aura-condition for Namtaru.

Languages: Mindless Unlife may have none. Intelligent forms may know Necril, Aklo, Undercommon, funerary cant, local ancestor-rite formulae, or the languages of those whose failed desires shaped them.

Environment: Negative-energy seams, failed shrines, underworld thresholds, lightless caverns, famine sites, grief-heavy settlements, ruined performance halls, abandoned wells, cold hearths, Shadowfell margins, and places where a necessary good has been ritually, socially, or cosmologically denied.

Organization: Solitary, pair, hunger, aura, failure, rite-clutch, deprivation field, paradox front, mourning house, or unliving bloom.
abilities:
Inverse Appetite (Su). An unliving creature desires a specific sustaining good, but receiving that good damages, shrinks, suppresses, banishes, or pacifies it. Deprivation of that good strengthens it. Each descendant must define the desired good, the harm caused by receiving it, and the benefit gained from deprivation.

Starvation Growth (Su). When denied its desired good, an unliving creature may gain temporary hit points, fast healing, increased size, stronger save DCs, restored uses of abilities, or a wider aura. This growth should be bounded by time, place, and degree of deprivation. It is not ordinary feeding.

Satisfaction Harm (Su). Giving the creature what it wants is a valid countermeasure. Bread harms the hunger-form. Water diminishes the unliving water-form. Song weakens the silence-fed joy-seeker if the song is sincere and complete. Lower taxa should specify whether this deals damage, suppresses abilities, forces a save, breaks regeneration, or causes retreat.

Necromantic Aura (Su). Most Unlife propagates a field that makes nearby reality resemble its deprivation: cold, thirst, silence, grief, darkness, stillness, spoilage, or failed offering. This aura may function like a negative-energy, fear, fatigue, cold, or curse effect depending on descendant. Its range, save, duration, and environmental signs must be stated locally.

Paradox Propagation (Su). Unlife reproduces by spreading its contradiction. A starving form makes food fail. A joy-seeker causes celebrations to sour. A light-seeker deepens darkness. A water-seeker desiccates wells. If the condition persists long enough, a new unliving seed, aura, echo, or body may form.

Negative Vitality (Su). Unlife is stabilized by negative-energy conditions and may resist death effects, energy drain, fatigue, or necromantic pressure. This is not identical to Undead healing. A descendant may be healed by negative energy, empowered by it, or merely made more coherent by it.

Positive or Fulfillment Disruption (Su). Positive energy, completed rites, sincere offerings, restored warmth, clean light, true joy, or successful nourishment can disrupt Unlife. The disrupting agent should match the inverse appetite. Generic positive energy may harm many forms, but the most reliable weakness is the fulfilled desire.

Contrary Element (Su). Paradox Elementals retain an apparent elemental appetite while expressing its negation. An unliving fire-form consumes the idea of fuel and radiates cold; an unliving water-form seeks water and spreads desiccation; an unliving air-form seeks breath and causes suffocation; an unliving earth-form seeks stone and leaves hollows. The desired element harms or diminishes the creature when actually supplied.

Rite Failure Sense (Su). Kispû and related Unlife can detect missed offerings, misnamed dead, broken ancestor obligations, empty tomb tables, spoiled libations, and funerary rites performed without true provision. This sense reveals failure, not truth in general.

Aura-Body Continuity (Su). Some Unlife, especially Namtaru, does not have a body separate from its field. Destroying the visible manifestation only matters if the deprivation-condition is also resolved. Otherwise the aura condenses again when hunger, grief, cold, darkness, or failed offering returns.

Merciful Feeding (Ex or Su). Field crews can sometimes weaken Unlife without combat by satisfying the desire correctly and fully. The act must be real, not symbolic mockery: actual water, actual warmth, actual song, actual offering, actual joy, actual recognition. Half-measures may strengthen the creature by sharpening deprivation.
bg:
Unlife matters because it punishes the wrong instincts. Against a vampire, feeding the hunger may empower the monster. Against an Akalû, feeding the hunger may be the cure. Against an undead fire elemental, water may be expected to oppose fire; against an unliving water paradox, water may be the very thing that collapses it. Field teams that treat Unlife as ordinary Undead often choose the exact wrong response.

The ancient logic of feeding the dead is especially important to the branch. In Mesopotamian and Egyptian funerary practice, offerings of food and drink sustained, nourished, or cared for the dead or their vital component. Unlife is the mythic inversion of that rule: the unoffered dead may become dangerous, but the unliving offering-form becomes dangerous because the offering is absent, spoiled, or impossible; when the offering is truly completed, the Unlife weakens.

For the Throat, Unlife is a route, shrine, and settlement hazard. Pall-Brokers, Heralds, shrine-keepers, ancestor-rite specialists, route-makers, and necromancy-trained surveyors must determine whether they face Undead, which usually requires burial, exorcism, destruction, release, or containment, or Unlife, which may require fulfillment: feed the hunger, light the hall, sing the song, complete the offering, restore the water, warm the hearth, or name the forgotten thing correctly.

The branch also clarifies why some hazards look impossible. A creature can seek joy and create tragedy because tragedy is its growth medium. A creature can seek sustenance while starving everything around it because starvation is its nourishment. A creature can be fire-shaped and cold because its fire is not fire’s life but fire’s unliving inverse.
g: Creatures that were never alive — animated by negative energy from their origin, not transformed from living creatures. Distinguished from Undead (which are transformations of living creatures) by the fact that no living ancestor exists in their lineage.
note: Corrected placement: Unlife is a pre-vital Inner-Primordial condition and is not derived from the Proto-Material species.
